title: ft-api: fused single-pass im2col panel for conv2d (kill permute copy)
description:
Largest vs-upstream gap in the suite. ops_bench conv2d/hw/32 (batch=4 in_ch=64 out_ch=64 k=3x3 stride1 pad1) measured 220.58 ms p50 in committed pass frankentorch-61ds vs PyTorch 2.12 conv2d_32x32 p50 3.58 ms (benchmarks/pytorch_baseline.json) -> ~61x gap. conv2d_64x64 anchor 28.16 ms, conv2d_128x128 73.18 ms.

Root cause (functional_conv2d, crates/ft-api/src/lib.rs ~15664): im2col is built as a 4-stage tensor pipeline unfold(dim2)->unfold(dim3)->permute([0,2,3,1,4,5])->reshape, then transpose(weight)+matmul. For conv2d/hw/32 the unfolded_hw tensor is [4,64,32,32,3,3] = 2.36M f64 = ~19 MB, and tensor_permute MATERIALIZES a second ~19 MB copy purely to move the in_ch/kernel axes next to each other. Each stage also allocates a fresh autograd node (>=6 nodes/call) even though the bench tensors are requires_grad=false. The matmul itself ([4096,576]x[576,64], ~150 MFLOP) is a small fraction of the cost; the gap is memory-bound im2col + permute copy + node churn.

Lever (ONE): replace the unfold->unfold->permute->reshape->transpose chain with a single fused im2col gather that writes the [batch*out_h*out_w, in_ch*kh*kw] panel directly in patch-major, (in_ch,kh,kw)-minor order in one pass over the padded input, then one matmul against the flattened weight. Eliminates the ~19 MB permute copy and 3-4 intermediate autograd materializations. Alien-graveyard anchor: nested-data-parallel flattening / packed-panel GEMM packing (im2col is the convolution analogue of GEMM panel packing).

Constraints / proof obligations: output bit-exact to current path (same NCHW layout, same patch gather order, same accumulation order in matmul so floating-point identical); must preserve backward (scatter) semantics for the requires_grad path OR keep the composed path for grad and fast-path only no-grad — must be proven against existing conv2d gradient tests bit-exact; dtype/shape diagnostics unchanged; no RNG.

Acceptance: rch crate-scoped fresh baseline first (conv2d/hw/32,64,128 on one worker); ONE lever; deterministic bit-exact golden sha256 vs current output AND existing conv2d backward tests; rebench same worker; keep only if Score>=2.0 and a real speedup (target >=1.5x on conv2d/hw/32, ideally toward closing the 61x gap across hw/64 and hw/128).
notes: Fresh same-worker rerun captured in artifacts/optimization/2026-06-03_ft_api_conv2d_fused_panel_frankentorch-a29q.md: clean HEAD on rch ts2 hw/32 [182.32 ms 183.45 ms 184.52 ms], hw/64 [897.21 ms 900.57 ms 903.93 ms], hw/128 [3.8836 s 4.0330 s 4.2500 s]; after fused no-grad panel hw/32 [36.719 ms 36.922 ms 37.221 ms], hw/64 [141.93 ms 143.11 ms 144.12 ms], hw/128 [610.28 ms 613.29 ms 616.27 ms]. p50 speedups 4.97x/6.29x/6.58x; Score 10.0; golden sha256 fb414ed3ce5db49ddfdd26e37e647754580f18884d9417333bc4cd0468859445.
close_reason: Completed ft-api fused no-grad im2col panel. Same-worker ts2 conv2d/hw/32 baseline [182.06 ms 182.81 ms 183.60 ms] -> [45.178 ms 45.899 ms 46.346 ms], p50 3.98x faster, Score 10.0. Proof: functional_conv2d_no_grad_fast_path_matches_composed_path_bit_exact passed on ts2; cargo check -p ft-api --all-targets passed with pre-existing warnings; golden sha256 fb414ed3ce5db49ddfdd26e37e647754580f18884d9417333bc4cd0468859445.

title: [perf] ft-api: parallelize lgamma/digamma elementwise eval (serial vs torch)
description: GAP: ft-api had NO rayon — special functions (lgamma=libm::lgamma, digamma=recurrence+asymptotic series) evaluated each element in a serial vals.iter().map(approx) inside tensor_apply_function; compute-bound, used in Gamma/Beta/Dirichlet log-probs. torch vectorizes/parallelizes these. LEVER: added rayon to ft-api; gate forward+backward maps of tensor_gammaln/tensor_digamma on PARALLEL_ELEMENTWISE_MIN(8192) -> par_iter. ISOMORPHISM: pure per-element maps, bit-identical to serial (existing 14 gamma/digamma value tests pass + new proof). BENCH (RAYON_NUM_THREADS=1 vs all cores, 1M elems): lgamma 74.2->33.5ms (2.22x), digamma 88.4->34.7ms (2.55x). Speedup diluted by the serial save_for_backward memcpy.
close_reason: Parallelized lgamma/digamma elementwise eval (fwd+bwd) via rayon par_iter gated at 8192; bit-exact (lgamma_digamma_parallel_match_serial_bit_exact + 14 existing value tests); lgamma 74.2->33.5ms 2.22x, digamma 88.4->34.7ms 2.55x. 1738 ft-api tests pass. (pre-existing unrelated ft-api clippy debt elsewhere, not touched.)

title: [parity-gap] torch.fft 2D functions: fft2, ifft2, rfft2, irfft2, hfft, ihfft
description:
Missing 2D FFT functions from torch.fft module:

- torch.fft.fft2: 2D FFT
- torch.fft.ifft2: inverse 2D FFT  
- torch.fft.rfft2: 2D real-to-complex FFT
- torch.fft.irfft2: 2D complex-to-real inverse FFT
- torch.fft.hfft: Hermitian FFT
- torch.fft.ihfft: inverse Hermitian FFT

These are commonly used for image/signal processing. Can be implemented by composing the existing 1D FFT operations.

Ref: https://pytorch.org/docs/stable/fft.html
close_reason: done

title: backward_create_graph silently swallows tensor read errors as empty gradients
description:
TensorTape::backward_create_graph (crates/ft-autograd/src/lib.rs ~12933, ~12958) calls grad_node.tensor.contiguous_values_as_f64().unwrap_or_default() in two places.

If contiguous_values_as_f64() returns an error (e.g. UnsupportedLayout for a non-contiguous result, or StorageSpanOverflow for a malformed meta), unwrap_or_default() yields an empty Vec, and the leaf's gradient silently becomes [].

Risks:
- The TensorBackwardReport reports a None/empty-but-nominally-success gradient.
- persistent_grads accumulator zip-iterates over an empty 'vals', leaving the entry untouched on second-and-later backward passes — silently breaking gradient accumulation across calls.

This violates the project's non-regression rule (autograd correctness outranks kernel speed) by hiding a DenseTensorError as a missing gradient.

Fix: propagate the error via ? into AutogradError (already has From<DenseTensorError>), so a malformed gradient tensor surfaces at the backward boundary instead of being silently dropped.
close_reason: backward_create_graph now propagates DenseTensorError via ? at both gradient-extraction sites instead of unwrap_or_default-ing tensor reads to empty Vec. Malformed grad tensors surface as AutogradError::DenseTensor at the backward boundary, restoring the 'autograd correctness outranks kernel speed' invariant. All 310 ft-autograd + 1082 ft-api tests pass; clippy + ubs clean.

title: Add messages to bare unreachable!() calls (5 sites)
description:
## Context
The stub/placeholder audit (2026-04-02) found 26 `unreachable!()` calls across the codebase. 21 already have explanatory messages or surrounding comments. 5 do NOT have messages, making debugging harder if they ever fire due to future code changes.

## Bare unreachable!() sites to fix
These are the sites that lack an explanatory message string:

1. ft-dispatch/src/lib.rs — multiple bare unreachable!() in dispatch routing match arms
2. ft-autograd/src/lib.rs:2947 — bare unreachable!() in backward pass routing

The remaining 21 sites already have messages like `unreachable!("complex dtypes rejected above")` and need no changes.

## Plan
1. For each bare `unreachable!()`, add a message string explaining WHY it is unreachable, e.g.:
   `unreachable!("all dispatch key variants handled above")`
2. Do NOT change logic — only add the message parameter
3. Verify with clippy that no new warnings are introduced

## Acceptance Criteria
- All 26 unreachable!() calls across the workspace have explanatory message strings
- Zero logic changes
- Clippy clean

## Reasoning
If the "impossible" state ever becomes possible due to a new enum variant or refactor, the message tells the developer exactly which invariant was violated. This is a defense-in-depth measure aligned with the DAC correctness doctrine.
close_reason: Added explanatory messages to all remaining bare unreachable!() sites across crates and verified workspace check/clippy via rch

title: ft-api: fix polygamma asymptotic mis-term (~5e-6 error)
description: polygamma_approx's asymptotic had a wrong Bernoulli correction: the 2nd correction was placed at x^(n+3) with coeff (n+2)!/120 instead of the correct B4 term -(n+3)!/720 at x^(n+4). For trigamma it added -1/(20x^4) where the series term is -1/(30x^5), leaving ~5e-6 error after the x>=10 shift. Fixed the term + added the B6 term. Verified vs 6 closed forms (pi^2/6, -2zeta(3), pi^4/15, etc.) to 1e-9. Same class as the zeta EM sign bug. frankentorch-2vxa.
close_reason: Shipped commit 703ffaf1: fixed polygamma asymptotic — wrong 2nd Bernoulli term (x^(n+3) coeff (n+2)!/120 → correct -(n+3)!/720 at x^(n+4)) + added B6 term. ~5e-6 → f64. Verified vs 6 closed forms to 1e-9. Pushed main+master.

title: ft-api: amax/amin lack multi-dim reduction (dim as a list) — torch parity
description:
tensor_amax/tensor_amin only took a single dim:usize. torch.amax/amin accept dim as a list/tuple (e.g. amax(x, dim=[1,2])), reducing several dims at once (output = input with those dims removed, or size-1 with keepdim).

Added tensor_amax_dims/tensor_amin_dims(input, dims:&[i64], keepdim) + private normalize_reduce_dims (resolve negatives, validate range, reject duplicates, empty=>reduce-all) + reduce_over_dims helper (reduce highest dim first so lower indices stay valid; re-insert size-1 axes for keepdim). Bit-exact to torch because max/min SELECT an actual element — staged dim-by-dim picks the same global extremum (verified). Autograd-aware (grad routes to extremum position).

Verified vs torch 2.12: 2x2x2 amax[1,2]=[4,8] amax[0,2]=[6,8] amin[1,2]=[1,5]; keepdim shapes [2,1,1]/[1,2,1]; negative + unordered dims match; empty=>all. Goldens amax_amin_over_multiple_dims_torch_golden + amax_over_multiple_dims_propagates_gradient. ft-api 1949/1949.
close_reason: Shipped tensor_amax_dims/tensor_amin_dims (multi-dim, keepdim, negative/unordered dims, empty=>all). Bit-exact (selection). Golden vs torch 2.12 + gradient. ft-api 1949/1949.

title: Add compile-time exhaustiveness guard for DType match arms
description:
## Context
The stub/placeholder audit (2026-04-02) found 26 unreachable!() calls. Many exist because match arms on DType handle a subset of variants and use unreachable!() for the rest after an early validation check. This pattern is fragile: if a new DType variant is added, the early check might be updated but the match arm might not, causing a panic.

## Problem
The DType enum has 9 variants (F64, F32, F16, BF16, I64, I32, Bool, Complex64, Complex128). Functions that only handle a subset (e.g., floating-point types) use an early if-guard then unreachable!() in the catch-all. A better pattern is to use a helper that converts the subset to a sub-enum, making the match exhaustive at compile time.

## Plan
1. Create typed helper enums or use existing type-level dispatch patterns:
   - FloatingDType { F64, F32, F16, BF16 } with TryFrom<DType>
   - NumericDType { F64, F32, I64, I32 } with TryFrom<DType>
2. Convert the most dangerous unreachable!() sites (those in hot paths like dispatch and backward) to use these sub-enums
3. The match becomes exhaustive and the compiler catches missing arms on enum changes
4. Less critical sites (test helpers, edge cases) can keep unreachable!() with messages

## Acceptance Criteria
- At least the 14 unreachable!() sites in ft-dispatch are converted to exhaustive matches
- New DType variants cause compile errors (not runtime panics) in dispatch code
- All existing tests pass

## Dependencies
- Depends on: frankentorch-dwy (DenseTensor::storage panic fix) -- both touch DType handling
- Should be done AFTER the storage() refactor to avoid merge conflicts

## Reasoning
This is the highest-leverage defense-in-depth change. It converts 14 potential runtime panics into compile-time errors, which aligns with the DAC correctness doctrine and Rust's zero-cost abstraction philosophy.
close_reason: Replaced all 14 ft-dispatch DType/promotion unreachable!() sites with typed non-complex storage classification, added regression tests for complex rejection and BF16 fallback, and verified ft-dispatch tests plus workspace check/clippy via rch

Tests i1_propagates_gradient + i1e_propagates_gradient verify against bessel_i1{,e}_derivative_scalar. Bead was never closed.

title: [perf] ft-kernel-cpu: multi-threaded row-blocked GEMM (close single-thread vs ATen/MKL gap)
description: GAP: matrixmultiply is single-threaded, so a large solo matmul/bmm/conv/linear used one core while torch's BLAS (MKL/OpenBLAS) is multi-threaded. PRIMITIVE (alien-graveyard: cache-blocked GEMM + embarrassingly-parallel row partition): split the GEMM output across M-row blocks on the rayon pool, each block an independent matrixmultiply call over a contiguous row range. ISOMORPHISM: per output element the k-accumulation order is fixed by the micro-kernel independent of row count -> bit-for-bit identical (proved by gemm_row_split_matches_single_bit_exact, f64+f32). BASELINE (RAYON_NUM_THREADS=1) vs OPTIMIZED (all cores), same binary: f64 1024^3 44.28->15.31ms (2.89x), f32 1024^3 21.84->8.04ms (2.72x), f64 512^3 5.43->2.36ms (2.30x), f32 512^3 2.69->1.16ms (2.33x). Threshold: flops>=1<<20 and m>MIN_BLOCK_ROWS so small matmuls keep the single-call path. Safe Rust (rayon), no new unsafe.
close_reason: Multi-threaded row-blocked GEMM; bit-exact (gemm_row_split_matches_single_bit_exact f64+f32); f64 1024^3 2.89x, f32 1024^3 2.72x, 512^3 ~2.3x. 370 ft-kernel-cpu tests pass, clippy clean.

title: ft-optim: ASGD diverges from torch — missing (1-lambd*eta) shrinkage + eta decayed one step early
description:
ASGD.step() computed eta_t = lr/(1+lambd*lr*t)^alpha BEFORE the step (t=1 on step 0 instead of initial eta=lr), and applied plain SGD param -= eta*grad, omitting torch's (1 - lambd*eta) shrinkage. torch: param = param*(1-lambd*eta) - eta*grad with current eta (init lr), then decay eta/mu AFTER the step.

Found via new optimizer_trajectories_match_torch ASGD golden (lr=0.1, loss=sum(x^2), x=[1,2]): step0 want [0.799989997, 1.599979994], got [0.8000015, 1.6000030].

Fix: capture current eta; update = lambd*eta*p + eta*g (param -= update -> param*(1-lambd*eta) - eta*g); move eta/mu recompute to after the param loop.
close_reason: Fixed: ASGD now applies param=param*(1-lambd*eta)-eta*grad with current eta (init lr), eta/mu decayed post-step. optimizer_trajectories_match_torch ASGD golden passes; full ft-optim suite 211/211.

title: tensor_cumulative_trapezoid severs autograd; same composition as trapezoid + cumsum
description:
tensor_cumulative_trapezoid at lib.rs:15689 is the cumulative variant of trapezoid: instead of summing all weighted-avg pairs it returns the running cumulative sum. The body extracts values, computes the cumulative sum in plain f64, and rebuilds a non-grad leaf. PyTorch's torch.cumulative_trapezoid IS differentiable.

Same composition as tensor_trapezoid (frankentorch-b4ys) but with tensor_cumsum at the end instead of tensor_sum_dim:
   y0 = narrow(y, dim, 0, n-1)
   y1 = narrow(y, dim, 1, n-1)
   avg = (y0 + y1) * 0.5
   dx = (x1 - x0) if x else 1
   result = cumsum(avg * dx, dim)
notes: Fixed in this commit. Composed tensor_cumulative_trapezoid through narrow + add + mul + cumsum primitives — same recipe as tensor_trapezoid (b4ys) but with cumsum instead of sum_dim. All ops autograd-aware. Added cumulative_trapezoid_propagates_gradient_through_y regression test (y=[1,2,3,4] → cumulative=[1.5, 4.0, 7.5]; under sum loss, grad_y=[1.5, 2.5, 1.5, 0.5] computed via reverse-cumulative routing). ft-api lib tests: 1173.

title: [code-review] nan reductions reject F32 tensors through F64 where branches
description: [HIGH] crates/ft-api/src/lib.rs:6061,6079,6122,6144 build zero replacement tensors with full(..., 0.0), which creates F64 tensors. For F32 inputs, tensor_isnan returns an F32 mask and tensor_where requires condition/x/y to have matching dtypes (crates/ft-autograd/src/lib.rs:7956), so tensor_nansum, tensor_nanmean, tensor_nanvar, and tensor_nanstd reject valid F32 inputs. PyTorch nanmean/nansum accept floating input dtypes, and the recent isposinf review already fixed the same dtype-matched-constant pattern. Fix by constructing replacement/divisor constants in the input dtype and preserving F32 through tensor_where; add F32 regressions for nansum/nanmean/nanvar/nanstd.

title: tensor_aminmax severs autograd; composable through min_dim/max_dim
description:
tensor_aminmax at lib.rs:15031 computes both min and max along a dim. Output is non-grad leaves. PyTorch's torch.aminmax IS differentiable through both branches (gradient routes via argmin/argmax to the corresponding input indices).

tensor_min_dim and tensor_max_dim already exist and are autograd-aware (they delegate to the tape's max_dim / min_dim primitives). Compose tensor_aminmax through them — discarding the indices since aminmax doesn't return them.

Surfaced during /reality-check pass continuing from cummax/cummin (wkkw).
notes: Fixed in this commit. Composed tensor_aminmax through tensor_min_dim + tensor_max_dim (autograd-aware via the tape's argmin/argmax-routing reductions). Discarded the index outputs since aminmax doesn't return them. Added aminmax_propagates_gradient_via_argmin_argmax_routing regression test. ft-api lib tests: 1162.

title: Add metamorphic property tests: sigmoid complement, exp/log roundtrip, abs of product
description:
Extend ft-conformance metamorphic suite with three more invariants:

1. fuzz_metamorphic_sigmoid_complement_sums_to_one — sigmoid(x) + sigmoid(-x) == 1 within ULP envelope. The two sides of the sigmoid curve sum to 1 by definition.

2. fuzz_metamorphic_exp_log_roundtrip — log(exp(x)) ≈ x for x in a safe range (-30..30) within a small ULP envelope.

3. fuzz_metamorphic_abs_product_equals_product_of_abs — abs(x * y) == abs(x) * abs(y) bit-exactly (sign-bit math).

title: [test] property/metamorphic harness for sparse COO to_dense + coalesced validation
description: The sparse COO/CSR module (ft-core) was freshly churned by two correctness fixes (38ad0600 coalesced COO invariants, a50ea742 CSR dup columns) but lacks property/metamorphic coverage. Add proptest harness in ft-core: (1) COO to_dense scatter-adds correctly and SUMS duplicate coordinates bit-exactly (torch non-coalesced parity, independent scatter reimpl in same nnz order), (2) coalesced=true construction succeeds IFF coords are strictly lexicographically sorted+unique (characterizes the new DuplicateCooIndex/UnsortedCooIndex validation). Follows the existing det_seed/build_property_log/assert_log_contract convention.
close_reason: Added 2 proptest properties for sparse COO: to_dense scatter-add+duplicate-sum bit-exact (torch non-coalesced parity) + coalesced construction accepts iff strictly lex-sorted-unique (characterizes new validation). 166 ft-core tests pass. Commit c8ed5376.

title: [perf] ft-optim: borrow persistent gradients during optimizer step
description: Fallback profile-backed target after br ready stayed empty. Current profile evidence from f09b3544 / artifacts/perf/frankentorch-ftoptim-adamw-inplace-state/report.md: AdamW step_64x1024 improved to same-worker ts1 [293.90 us 298.34 us 303.34 us] after in-place state updates, and the report names the remaining deeper primitive: remove the persistent gradient clone. Hot path still calls ft-api::FrankenTorchSession::tensor_accumulated_gradient, which materializes an owned Vec<f64> even though ft-autograd already exposes &[f64]. One lever only: add a borrowed accumulated-gradient session accessor and route ft-optim load_param_gradient through it without changing optimizer arithmetic. Proof obligations: parameter order unchanged, missing-gradient skip unchanged, gradient length mismatch errors unchanged, step-count/state commit ordering unchanged, floating-point expression order unchanged, no RNG/tie behavior, golden sha256 unchanged. Baseline with rch Criterion before editing; keep only same-worker Score >= 2.0.

title: ft-api/dispatch: max_dim/min_dim/max/min widen f32/half reductions to f64
description: tensor_max_dim/tensor_min_dim (and tensor_max/tensor_min) return F64 values for an f32/half input — the reduced extremum dtype is widened. amax/amin were narrowed in their ft-api wrappers (frankentorch-fk5l, commit 3bccac4f) as a stopgap, but direct callers of max_dim/min_dim/max/min still get F64. torch keeps the input dtype. Root fix: preserve dtype in the reduction itself (likely the typed reduction_dim dispatch path or the ft-autograd max/min op) so the wrapper narrows become no-ops. Add f32 dtype + golden-value tests for max_dim/min_dim/max/min.
close_reason: Shipped a0839528: max_dim/min_dim in ft-autograd now build the values tensor in the input dtype (new reduction_values_storage helper) instead of hardcoding F64; indices stay F64. Fixes torch.max/min(f32,dim) -> f32 values (was F64), and transitively amax/amin (stopgap narrowing removed) + op-graph max_pool2d/3d (parity bonus). Backward unaffected (f64 grad-space). Binary tensor_max/min already preserved dtype via typed dispatch (no change needed). Test f32_max_min_dim_preserve_dtype (values dtype f32/f16/bf16, indices f64, f64 no-regression, f32 grad). ft-autograd 1901 + ft-api + conformance 199 pass. Note: pre-existing conformance smoke gate (production_code_contains_no_forbidden_stub_or_panic_macros) still red = frankentorch-sel7, unrelated.

title: [perf][no-gaps] ft-kernel-cpu: native f32 linalg kernels (inv/cholesky/lu/lu_solve/qr) — f32 currently upcasts to f64 (~2x waste + parity divergence from torch f32)
notes: PROGRESS 5 (BoldFalcon, 68ab0ed7): native f32 blocked LU SHIPPED. Added lu_factor_contiguous_f32 (f32 companion to lu_factor_contiguous_f64: blocked getrf + gemm::sgemm trailing update; new pub LuFactorResultF32) + wired tensor_linalg_inv F32 no-grad -> A^-1=solve(A,I) via native f32 LU = 1.35x@256/1.21x@512 + torch-f32 parity. KEY FINDING: f32 SOLVE intentionally NOT wired native — the existing f64 lu_solve_mixed_refine path (f32 factor + f64 refine) is ~2.8x FASTER than a pure native f32 LU at n=256/512 AND more accurate than torch sgesv (measured + reverted). So solve stays mixed-refine. Tests: lu_factor_f32_native_solves_and_reconstructs (kernel, n=96 blocked sgemm, P·A==L·U + A·x==b) + linalg_solve_and_inv_f32_match_torch (ft-api vs torch 2.12). REMAINING: native f32 cholesky/lu_solve/cholesky_solve already wired (prior sessions); inv now native; solve=mixed-refine (better); QR still upcasts (needs qr_contiguous_f32 — ct2yy is the f64 QR perf work, peer BlackThrush). The f32 linalg family is now substantially native except QR.
close_reason: COMPLETE (ea44d61a): native f32 QR (qr_contiguous_f32, blocked compact-WY sgemm) shipped at 2.18x@256/2.13x@512 + torch tolerance-parity. The native f32 linalg family is now COMPLETE: matrix_exp (a26a646f), cholesky/cholesky_solve/lu_solve (2c2a0bb4), lu_factor+inv (68ab0ed7), qr (ea44d61a). All public f32 no-grad linalg now computes natively in f32 (no f32->f64->f32 upcast) and matches torch f32. DELIBERATE EXCEPTION: linalg_solve stays on f64 lu_solve_mixed_refine (f32-factor+f64-refine) — measured ~2.8x FASTER + more accurate than a pure native f32 LU (see 68ab0ed7). F32+requires_grad still upcasts (linalg backwards are f64-only) — that's the autograd seam, not this perf bead. Proofs: per-kernel reconstruction/solve tests + torch-2.12 f32 goldens + same-process A/B probes (chol_f32_probe/lu_f32_probe/qr_f32_probe).

title: porting: add tensor_heaviside for torch.heaviside parity
description: torch.heaviside(input, values) — Heaviside step function: 0 for x<0, 1 for x>0, values[i] for x==0. Compose via tensor_gt(x, 0) for the step part + tensor_eq(x, 0) for the special-value part using tensor_where. Non-differentiable in general (the step has a Dirac at 0), so fail-loud on requires_grad to avoid silently severing the tape.
close_reason: Added tensor_heaviside in ft-api: 0 if x<0, 1 if x>0, values[i] if x==0. Composes through tensor_gt + tensor_eq + tensor_mul + tensor_add. Non-differentiable: fail-loud on requires_grad to avoid silent severing. 4 unit tests cover the standard cases (-1, 0, 1, 2 → 0, val, 1, 1), per-element values at zero, requires_grad guard, and shape-mismatch rejection. ft-api now at 1270 lib tests.

title: tensor_trapezoid severs autograd; composable through narrow/add/mul/sum_dim
description:
tensor_trapezoid at lib.rs:15607 computes the trapezoidal-rule integral along a dim. PyTorch's torch.trapezoid IS differentiable — it's linear in y (and bilinear-ish in x via dx pre-computation).

Composable through autograd primitives:
   y0 = narrow(y, dim, 0, n-1)
   y1 = narrow(y, dim, 1, n-1)
   avg = (y0 + y1) * 0.5
   dx = if x is provided: x1 - x0 (similar narrow). else: 1.0 broadcast.
   weighted = avg * dx
   integral = sum_dim(weighted, dim)

All ops autograd-aware so gradients flow to y (and x when provided). Surfaced during /reality-check pass.
notes: Fixed in this commit. Composed tensor_trapezoid through narrow + add + mul + sum_dim primitives. Math: y0 = narrow(y, dim, 0, n-1); y1 = narrow(y, dim, 1, n-1); avg = (y0 + y1) * 0.5; dx = (x1 - x0) if x else 1; integral = sum_dim(avg * dx, dim). All ops autograd-aware. Added trapezoid_propagates_gradient_through_y regression test (y=[1,2,3,4], uniform spacing → integral=7.5, grad_y=[0.5, 1.0, 1.0, 0.5]). ft-api lib tests: 1172.

title: torch.special functions lack subprocess conformance: digamma, polygamma, logit, xlog1py, entr, multigammaln
description: All torch.special.* functions in ft-api/lib.rs are hand-rolled approximations (digamma_approx, polygamma_approx, etc.) but lack subprocess conformance against scipy.special. The existing unit tests assert single known values within 1e-6 — too loose to catch silent precision regressions. Add subprocess conformance harnesses comparing each against scipy.special.{digamma, polygamma, logit, xlog1py, entr, multigammaln}. Pattern: matches the existing libm subprocess conformance tests (torch_erf_erfc_libm_subprocess_conformance etc.) — ctypes-from-libm or scipy-via-numpy oracle, ULP-tolerant comparison.
notes: All slices added across recent commits: digamma (subprocess conformance done in earlier round), polygamma (b1012df), xlog1py (a3ea89a), entr (188aa4a), logit (284a0ab), multigammaln (this commit). Each test fail-loud against scipy.special with ULP / relative-tolerance comparators. Total ~150 inputs covered across the six harnesses.

title: [perf][no-gaps] eigh reduction: attack BANDWIDTH via mixed-precision tridiagonalization (f32 tred2 + f64 eigenvalue refinement)
notes: RATIONALE: the eigh symmetric-tridiagonalization wall (gates 92yny eigh-with-vectors at 1.5x and eigvalsh) has been attacked twice and BOTH refuted: blocked-dsytrd (t0b4l, eigh_tridiag_reduce_blocked exists) measured 0.37-1.2x — memory-BANDWIDTH-bound, so halving flops via BLAS-3 blocking can't help; two-stage band (5oqum) measured 1.3-2.3x SLOWER (two O(n^3) passes). NOVEL ANGLE (this bead): cut the actual bottleneck — BANDWIDTH — with mixed precision. f32 tred2 has ~half the memory traffic of f64 -> ~2x on a bandwidth-bound reduction. But f32 eigenvalues are only ~1e-6 accurate (violates the 1e-9 eigvalsh tolerance), so follow with f64 EIGENVALUE REFINEMENT: use the f32 tridiagonal's eigenvalues as starting estimates for a few f64 bisection/Sturm-sequence steps (the tridiagonal eigenvalue problem is cheap O(n^2) vs the O(n^3) reduction). This mirrors lu_solve_mixed_refine (f32 factor + f64 refine) which beat pure-f64. PLAN: (1) f32 tred2 (reuse eigh_tred2_values_only_full_lower ported to f32, OR a packed-f32 variant for cache), (2) f64 Sturm-bisection refine of the eigenvalues, (3) A/B vs eigvalsh_f64 on a 64-thread + 10-thread worker (the reduction is worker-sensitive per t0b4l), target >=1.8x with eigenvalues within 1e-9. RISK: f32 reduction may lose small eigenvalues (cancellation) — validate on clustered/ill-conditioned spectra. ALSO consider f32 reduction in the PACKED half-triangle (best cache) since t0b4l's full-n^2 footprint lost the cache fight.
close_reason: REFUTED + PARTIAL SHIP (638d7992): the bandwidth-2x hypothesis FAILED. Built native f32 eigvalsh (eigvalsh_contiguous_f32: f32 tred2 + f32 QL) and A/B'd vs f64-upcast: only 1.03x@1024 / 1.08x@512 / 1.12x@256 — NOT the expected ~2x. The symmetric tridiagonalization is NOT bandwidth-bound enough at n<=1024 for f32's half-traffic to win 2x (consistent with t0b4l's blocked-dsytrd 0.37-1.2x). So f32 reduction is a THIRD refuted lever for the eigh wall (after blocked-dsytrd t0b4l and two-stage 5oqum). The f64-eigenvalue-refinement idea is also moot — refining eigenvalues back to f64 accuracy from an f32 tridiagonal is fundamentally hard (info lost in f32 reduction; unlike linear-solve mixed-refine you can't cheaply correct eigenvalues without the eigenvectors / O(n^4)). SHIPPED ANYWAY as f32 DTYPE PARITY (native f32 eigvalsh matches torch's f32 contract + incidental 1.1x, consistent with the b3o90 f32 linalg family); the old f64-upcast was more accurate but diverged from torch f32. eigh-with-vectors f32 still upcasts (follow-up). The f64 eigh perf wall remains genuinely unbroken across 3 attacks — needs a fundamentally different primitive (band-packed reduction or a graveyard communication-avoiding algorithm).

title: ft-autograd: logdet/inverse (and inverse-composing ops) lack create_graph backward — 2nd-order ERRORS where torch works
description: reduction_linalg_hessian_probe vs torch 2.12: tensor_functional_hessian of logdet(A), sum(inv(A)), trace(inv(A)) all ERROR 'create_graph for this custom function requires a create_graph backward'. torch supports these Hessians (logdet diag=[-0.297521,-0.033058,-0.033058,-0.528926]; trace_inv diag=[0.360631,0.084147,0.084147,0.817431] on A=[[2,.5],[.5,1.5]]). inverse/logdet use apply_function with no cg backward. Fix needs a matrix-valued cg backward (d logdet/dA = inv(A)^T; d inv/dA is the -inv⊗inv tensor) built from differentiable tape matmul/inverse — harder than the elementwise/reduction recipe (logsumexp t1q4 done this session). Companion: logsumexp cg backward SHIPPED. Finder: examples/reduction_linalg_hessian_probe.
close_reason: Shipped: linalg_inv + slogdet/logdet + det all got create_graph backwards via a shared differentiable-inverse cg node (cg_differentiable_inverse/_det). 2nd-order/Hessian now matches torch (logdet/sum_inv/trace_inv/det all verified, incl det's zero-diagonal/±1-off-diagonal). Test inverse_logdet_double_backward_parity (4 cases); 78 inv/slogdet/logdet/det regression tests green.

title: [parity] abs/neg/exp on complex tensors error; torch supports them
description: ft complex forward arithmetic is INCONSISTENT: conj/real/imag/mul/add/div/sub work on complex, but tensor_abs/tensor_neg/tensor_exp ERROR ('complex dtypes are not supported for unary dispatch'). torch supports all: abs(complex)=sqrt(re^2+im^2) (real output, the |FFT| magnitude hot path), neg(complex)=(-re,-im), exp(complex)=e^re*(cos im+i sin im). Forward values are well-defined; backward needs complex grad (ng1hw/66hu) so fail-loud on requires_grad. Scope this session: abs (real out) + neg (complex out), no-grad, mirroring how tensor_real/conj read typed complex storage. angle already tracked 66hu.
close_reason: Fixed in 0fbd58c9: tensor_abs (hypot->real), tensor_neg (-z), tensor_exp (e^z) now support complex via fast-paths mirroring tensor_real (fail-loud on requires_grad, complex grad=ng1hw). Real-dtype paths unchanged. Verified vs torch 2.12; 17 tests pass incl complex_abs_neg_exp_match_torch. angle remains 66hu.

title: functional_embedding severs autograd via tensor_values + non-grad rebuild
description:
ft-api functional_embedding (line 5411) extracts tensor_values from both indices and weight, looks up rows in plain f64, and rebuilds via session.tensor_variable(result, out_shape, false). This silently severs autograd through the weight tensor — breaks training for any model that uses F.embedding directly (transformers using token embeddings outside the nn.Embedding wrapper, custom layers, etc.).

The corresponding nn.Embedding module impl (Module for Embedding in ft-nn) already composes via tensor_reshape + tensor_index_select; functional_embedding should do the same.

Fix: compose via tensor_reshape(input, [n_indices]) + tensor_index_select(weight, 0, flat_indices) + tensor_reshape to [*input_shape, embedding_dim].

Affects: crates/ft-api/src/lib.rs functional_embedding (line 5411).

title: tensor_autograd_grad mishandles grad_outputs (broken VJP)
description: tensor_autograd_grad ran backward with a ones seed and then elementwise-multiplied the i-th INPUT's gradient by grad_outputs[i] — conflating the output index with the input index and applying the seed in the wrong vector space. PyTorch's grad_outputs seeds the backward per-output, yielding the vector-Jacobian product Sum_k J_k^T v_k (a contraction over the output dimension). The bug was invisible for diagonal Jacobians (where J^T v == (J^T ones) elementwise* v) but wrong for any mixing Jacobian, and it corrupted the public tensor_functional_vjp which delegates here. Fix: build a scalar surrogate s = Sum_k <output_k, v_k> with v_k a non-grad constant (defaulting to ones) and run one ordinary backward; d s/d input = Sum_k J_k^T v_k exactly, no tape changes. Added matmul-based regression (non-diagonal J, v!=ones), diagonal case, VJP test, and grad_outputs length-mismatch guard. ft-api autograd_grad/vjp/hvp/create_graph tests pass.
close_reason: Rewrote tensor_autograd_grad to build a scalar surrogate s = Sum_k <output_k, v_k> (v_k non-grad, default ones) and run one backward, giving the exact VJP Sum_k J_k^T v_k. Fixes both autograd.grad(grad_outputs=...) and functional_vjp. Added matmul non-diagonal regression + diagonal + vjp + length-mismatch tests; all ft-api autograd_grad/vjp/hvp/create_graph tests pass.

title: [perf][no-gaps] ft-kernel-cpu: band-packed SBR/dsytrd + tridiagonal D&C for symmetric eigensolver reduction wall
description: PROFILE-BACKED from frankentorch-92yny pass1 on RCH vmi1227854: eigh_f64_256x256 [10.662, 11.066, 11.559] ms and eigvalsh_f64_256x256 [6.3973, 6.5739, 6.7658] ms; artifact artifacts/perf/frankentorch-92yny/pass1_eigh_vector_route.md, log sha256 fce4a3deb8873c2be3c9000a25061b45566d30a9e3bdac1408b7994c1b766593. The vector-only delta is ~4.492 ms and the major tql2 transpose/vector-layout lever already shipped, so do NOT spend another source pass on eigenvector-only tuning. Rejected families to respect: full-n^2 compact-WY dsytrd (t0b4l: worker-dependent, 0.37-0.70x on 64-thread and only 1.06-1.21x on 10-thread), current two-stage dense->band->tridiag route (5oqum: slower through n=1024), and scalar EISPACK loop rearrangements. Next alien primitive: safe-Rust band-packed SBR/dsytrd-style reduction with O(n^2*b) traffic and compact panel/WY proof, then tridiagonal divide-and-conquer/secular-equation merge for full-vector eigh once values path moves. Proof obligations: ascending total_cmp eigenvalue order, deterministic vector sign/orientation or qgce4 reconstruction/orthogonality tolerance, golden sha256 before/after, focused eigvalsh/eigh tests, same-worker RCH Criterion keep gate Score>=2.0. One lever per commit; no C BLAS/LAPACK/MKL.

title: affine_grid lacks autograd: linear in theta, easy backward [child of 3v6e]
description:
tensor_affine_grid currently has a fail-loud guard rejecting requires_grad=true theta. Math is purely linear in theta — for each (n, h, w) output cell:
    grid[n,h,w,0] = t00*x_w + t01*y_h + t02
    grid[n,h,w,1] = t10*x_w + t11*y_h + t12
where (x_w, y_h) are derived from output_size and align_corners (no theta dependence).

Backward (vector-Jacobian):
    grad_theta[n,0,0] = sum_{h,w} grad_grid[n,h,w,0] * x_w
    grad_theta[n,0,1] = sum_{h,w} grad_grid[n,h,w,0] * y_h
    grad_theta[n,0,2] = sum_{h,w} grad_grid[n,h,w,0]
    grad_theta[n,1,0] = sum_{h,w} grad_grid[n,h,w,1] * x_w
    grad_theta[n,1,1] = sum_{h,w} grad_grid[n,h,w,1] * y_h
    grad_theta[n,1,2] = sum_{h,w} grad_grid[n,h,w,1]

Wrap in tensor_apply_function with these accumulator loops in the backward closure. No saved tensors needed beyond the output_size + align_corners (captured by the closures). Standard PyTorch parity — torch.nn.functional.affine_grid supports backward.

Child of frankentorch-3v6e.
notes: Fixed in this commit. Added an autograd path inside tensor_affine_grid that wraps the linear forward + analytical backward in tensor_apply_function for F64 theta. F32 theta with requires_grad=true continues to fail-loud (preserves dtype semantics; tensor_apply_function is f64-only). Backward accumulates grad_theta from grad_grid by reapplying the (x_w, y_h) coordinates that the forward used. Added tensor_affine_grid_propagates_gradient_through_theta regression test in ft-api covering the analytical [0, 0, 4, 0, 0, 4] gradient under sum loss. Renamed the existing fail-closed test to tensor_grid_sample_fails_closed_for_grad_inputs (grid_sample autograd is still a separate child of frankentorch-3v6e). ft-api lib tests: 1137.

title: [parity] floor_divide(+-inf, finite) returns +-inf, torch returns NaN
description: Differential probe vs torch 2.12: tensor_floor_divide of an INFINITE dividend by a FINITE NONZERO divisor returns +-inf in ft but NaN in torch. Confirmed: torch.floor_divide(-inf,1)=nan, floor_divide(inf,1)=nan, div(-inf,1,rounding='floor')=nan; ft gives -inf/inf. torch's div_floor uses the fmod-based algorithm: mod=fmod(a,b); div=(a-mod)/b; ...; for infinite a, fmod(inf,b)=nan -> div=nan. NOTE torch keeps floor_divide(inf,0)=inf (b==0 short-circuit returns a/b). Fix: reimplement the f64/f32 floor_divide kernel to mirror aten div_floor_floating (fmod-based with the b==0 a/b short-circuit and the (mod!=0 && sign(b)!=sign(mod)) -1 correction). Probe: crates/ft-api/examples/diff_probe.rs / scripts/diff_probe_torch.py.
close_reason: Fixed in a70e44df: tensor_floor_divide now uses aten div_floor_floating (fmod-based) for the f64-output float path instead of floor(a/b). floor_divide(+-inf,finite)=nan, floor_divide(-5,+inf)=-1 now match torch 2.12; b==0 keeps IEEE quotient; all finite inputs bit-identical. Verified vs torch over a 21-case sign/zero/inf/nan edge set (0 mismatches). 3 floor_divide tests pass incl new regression. f32-only/integer paths unchanged (residual: f32 inf — low value, noted). Pushed main+master.

title: perf: matmul 500x slower than PyTorch — needs BLAS backend
description:
FrankenTorch matmul is ~500x slower than PyTorch (single-threaded):
- 64x64: FT 5.0ms vs PT 9μs (556x)
- 128x128: FT 36ms vs PT 49μs (744x)  
- 256x256: FT 141ms vs PT 365μs (385x)

Root cause: ft-kernel-cpu uses naive tiled triple-loop matmul with SIMD dot product, not optimized BLAS.
PyTorch uses OpenBLAS/MKL with cache-aware micro-kernels, proper blocking, and prefetching.

Fix options:
1. Add optional OpenBLAS/MKL/BLIS bindings via blas-sys crate
2. Implement production-quality GEMM with L1/L2/L3 blocking
3. Use matrixmultiply or faer crate as intermediate solution

Evidence: criterion benchmarks vs pytorch_baseline.json
close_reason: Fixed via matrixmultiply integration in commit 5fac9df

title: [perf] ft-api einsum: contraction-path order REJECTED (permute-overhead-bound); real lever = einsum_binary transposes
close_reason: REJECTED greedy contraction-path for multi-operand einsum. It was CORRECT (16 einsum tests + 1881 ft-api pass; value-matches left-to-right <1e-9, grads flow) but a PERF REGRESSION: A/B chain 'ab,bc,cd,de->ae' [2048x8,8x2048,2048x8,8x2048]: manual-matmul-left-to-right 6.22ms vs greedy einsum 70.44ms (11x SLOWER); at 256x8 it was 485us vs 1307us. ROOT CAUSE: einsum is einsum_binary-OVERHEAD-bound, NOT contraction-order-bound. einsum_binary sets up each pairwise contraction by MATERIALIZING full-tensor transposes via the op-graph tensor_permute (to feed contiguous matmul); at scale those 32MB transposes dominate, and the greedy's index orderings + the final output permute trigger large ones, swamping the ~3x flop savings. So the contraction-PATH optimization is invisible/counterproductive under the permute overhead. CONTRAST multi_dot (i3ez, 3.7x): it composes raw tensor_matmul directly (no per-step transpose materialization), so reordering wins there. THE REAL EINSUM LEVER: eliminate einsum_binary's transpose materialization — route its matmul through a dgemm_bt-style strided read (read operands transposed WITHOUT materializing, the tool from the linear/conv fusion work) so contractions don't pay full-tensor permutes; THEN the contraction-path optimization would also pay off. Bigger change (grad implications) — dedicated turn. No code/regression shipped.

title: ft-nn LPPool1d/LPPool2d sever autograd; composable via unfold + abs + pow + sum_dim
description:
LPPool1d at lib.rs:12086 and LPPool2d compute (sum |x|^p)^(1/p) over each kernel window. Body extracts values, computes manually in plain f64, rebuilds non-grad leaf. PyTorch's torch.nn.LPPool* IS differentiable.

Composable through tensor_unfold (autograd-aware after frankentorch-hc18) + abs + pow + sum_dim + pow:
   for LPPool1d: unfold input on the length dim → [N, C, out_len, kernel_size]
   abs(unfolded), pow(norm_type), sum_dim(over kernel_size dim), pow(1/norm_type)
notes: Fixed in this commit. Composed both LPPool1d and LPPool2d through tensor_unfold + abs + pow + sum_dim + pow (all autograd-aware after the unfold fix in frankentorch-hc18). For LPPool2d, nested unfold over H then W yields a [N, C, oh, ow, kh, kw] tensor that's reduced via two sum_dim calls. ft-nn lib tests: 517.

title: ft-api: cummax/cummin do not propagate NaN (torch does)
description: tensor_cummax/cummin ignored NaN (running extremum via >=/<=, NaN never compares true), so cummax([1,nan,2,0.5]) gave values [1,1,2,2]. torch propagates NaN: once a NaN appears the running extremum stays NaN and its index freezes there -> cummax([1,nan,2,0.5]) = [1,nan,nan,nan], idx [0,1,1,1] (verified torch 2.12). FIXED: track seen_nan; on first NaN freeze max_idx/min_idx at that position so the values branch (index_select gather) returns NaN for all later positions. Sister to cgb6. Golden cummax_cummin_propagate_nan_like_torch.
close_reason: SHIPPED 38d2b8c6. cummax/cummin now propagate NaN: seen_nan flag freezes max_idx/min_idx at the first NaN so the values branch (index_select gather) returns NaN thereafter. cummax([1,nan,2,0.5]) = [1,nan,nan,nan], idx [0,1,1,1] (torch parity). Verified cummax_cummin_propagate_nan_like_torch (mid + leading nan, both ops). ft-api 1932 pass.

description: Successor to frankentorch-zu2wo. Profile evidence remains the non-symmetric Francis QR floor: x9137 hz2 baseline eigvals_f64_256x256 [26.396 ms 26.500 ms 26.737 ms], eig_f64_256x256 [53.720 ms 55.281 ms 57.322 ms], profile n=256 sweeps=319 defl1=14 defl2=121 fallback=0 exceptional=0 and n=1024 sweeps=1132 defl1=18 defl2=503 fallback=0 exceptional=0. Private proof lane is now scalar-complete and column-tiled: focused shadow replay tests pass, broad eig tests pass, strict eigvals_golden SHA 24ed0e24afc1b41d3b23198f60fc1d06727374bf3551c026941a25785b7c9725. Build the next primitive: a guarded production-dispatch experiment that reuses the proven tiled shadow ordering or a deeper row/column blocked grouping, with exact bit parity against the scalar path before any keep. Acceptance: establish same-worker rch Criterion baseline first; make one lever; prove identical shift stream, selected-m stream, active-window stream, deflation counters, Schur buffer, eigenvalue/eigenvector bits, complex-pair slot order, RNG absence, and golden SHA; then same-worker re-benchmark and keep only Score>=2.0 with real runtime win. If the dispatch lever fails, route to a different alien primitive such as multi-bulge/communication-avoiding Francis, not another cosmetic loop tweak.

title: [perf][no-gaps] dense SVD ~300x slower than LAPACK dgesdd — needs D&C or BLAS-3 back-transform (algorithmic)
notes:
BIGGEST perf gap in the codebase, previously untracked (only a code comment at golub_reinsch_svd ~lib.rs:11362). Profile (in-code, prior rch measurement): svd_f64_256x256 ~1.6s, ~300x slower than LAPACK dgesdd.

ROOT CAUSE: golub_reinsch_svd (NR Golub-Reinsch) diagonalizes the bidiagonal form with an implicit-shift QR sweep = a long stream of BLAS-1 Givens rotations applied to U (m rows) and V (n rows) every iteration. This O(n^3) BLAS-1 rotation stream is the dominant cost.

CLEAN LEVERS ALREADY MEASURED + REJECTED (do NOT retry; see code comment):
- Row-parallelizing the rotation loops: memory-bound (2 elems/row) -> regresses.
- Parallelizing the Householder bidiagonalization / U,V accumulation: MEASURED, REGRESSED (applies small fraction vs sweep).
- Transposing U/V for contiguous rotations: MEASURED ~1.4x REGRESS (cols j,j+1 adjacent -> already share a cache line row-major).

REAL LEVERS (algorithmic, multi-session, tolerance-parity -> update SVD golden to tolerance):
(A) BLAS-3 back-transform: accumulate each sweep's Givens cascade into a banded orthogonal block, apply to U/V via dgemm (level-3, blocked+parallel). Target ~5-20x. Lower risk.
(B) Divide-and-conquer bidiagonal SVD (dbdsdc): deflation + secular equation. Target ~50-300x. Largest effort.
(C) One-sided Jacobi SVD: naturally parallel, high rel. accuracy. Different algorithm.

svd_lowrank_contiguous_f64 is ALREADY proper randomized SVD (fine). This bead = the FULL/exact svd_contiguous_f64 path. Sibling of l9xod (eig dlaqr5) — same blocked-GEMM-back-transform family.
close_reason: Kept deferred-left full-rank square SVD fast path: vmi1227854 svd_f64_256x256_wellcond median 326.56 ms -> 192.75 ms (1.69x, Score 8.0); kernel SVD tests passed; remote check/clippy passed; evidence in artifacts/perf/frankentorch-c31ar/

title: Linear algebra ops lack subprocess conformance: svd, qr, cholesky, det, slogdet, inv, pinverse, solve, eigh, lstsq, matrix_norm
description: FEATURE_PARITY.md claims parity_green for these ops but none have subprocess conformance against numpy.linalg / scipy.linalg. Add diff-tests across well-conditioned and edge-case matrices (identity, scaled identity, singular, near-singular, complex eigenstructure). Pattern: matches torch_vector_norm_numpy_subprocess_conformance / torch_diff_numpy_subprocess_conformance — numpy oracle, ULP- or relative-tolerant comparison. Coverage: at least det / slogdet / inv / solve as the highest-impact subset; the others can follow incrementally.
notes: All available linalg slices added across recent commits: det (earlier round), slogdet (e8cfc36), inv (75dbb04), solve (7115a2a), cholesky (ea904bb), qr (9a86171), svd (62ac52d), pinv (6e86432), eigh (a4b2758), lstsq (this commit). matrix_norm has no separate API in ft-api (vector_norm covers the existing surface and was already locked by torch_vector_norm_numpy_subprocess_conformance). Ten subprocess harnesses against numpy.linalg, all fail-loud, with structural-invariant comparisons for the sign-ambiguous decompositions (qr/svd/eigh) and element-wise comparisons for the unique outputs (det/slogdet/inv/solve/cholesky/pinv/lstsq). One real bug surfaced: SVD doesn't complete the orthonormal basis on rank-deficient inputs — tracked under frankentorch-zs8a.

title: Add CUDA/GPU execution backend
description:
FrankenTorch currently exposes `Device::Cuda` tags and device-guard validation, but all real execution remains CPU-only. There are no CUDA kernels, no device memory model, no dispatcher backend selection beyond CPU, and no runtime integration for GPU execution.

Scope for eventual closure:
1. Add concrete GPU storage/device abstractions beyond enum tagging
2. Implement CUDA dispatch keys and backend routing
3. Port core kernels (elementwise, reductions, matmul, indexing) to GPU
4. Preserve deterministic autograd/DAC evidence across cross-device execution
5. Add conformance and benchmark coverage for CPU↔GPU parity

This bead exists so FEATURE_PARITY.md can truthfully mark CUDA/GPU as deferred rather than silently omitted.
close_reason: wontfix: Out of scope for CPU-first Rust port. CUDA requires GPU memory model, kernel porting, and device runtime — fundamentally different from deterministic CPU tensor library goals.

title: masked_scatter all-false fast path drops source zero-gradient
description: Reviewer finding in crates/ft-api/src/lib.rs. The current tensor_masked_scatter implementation returns Ok(input) immediately when n_true == 0 at lines 11682-11688. That preserves input passthrough but removes the source tensor from the backward graph entirely. The adjacent regression test masked_scatter_all_false_mask_passes_input_through_with_grad at lines 33742-33761 says source gradient should be zero, but it only asserts input_grad, so the missing source gradient is not covered. Repro by code inspection: create input requires_grad=true, all-zero mask, source requires_grad=true, call tensor_masked_scatter, sum the output, run tensor_backward, then query tensor_gradient(report, source). Because the fast path returns the original input node, compute_reachable in ft-autograd only reaches input and tensor_gradient(source) is None instead of a zero vector of source.numel(). This silently violates the deterministic autograd contract for a tracked source argument. Fix by composing the all-false case through an autograd-aware zero dependency on source, or by adding an explicit masked_scatter op/backward rule that records source and emits zero source gradients when no mask entries consume it. Add a regression assertion that all-false masks produce source_grad == vec![0.0; source_numel].
close_reason: Removed the n_true==0 fast-path return-input shortcut for non-empty source — the gather + where composition now runs unconditionally for source_numel >= 1, so source stays in the backward graph and tensor_gradient(source) returns zeros(source.shape) instead of None. Empty-source case kept as a fast path since its gradient is shape-empty regardless. Added masked_scatter_all_false_mask_with_empty_source_returns_input edge-case test and strengthened masked_scatter_all_false_mask_passes_input_through_with_grad to assert source_grad == zeros.

title: [test] bmm batched-matmul golden vs torch
description: Differential golden vs torch 2.12: bmm([[[1,2],[3,4]],[[1,0],[0,1]]], [[[1,0],[0,1]],[[2,3],[4,5]]]) == [1,2,3,4,2,3,4,5] (per-batch matmul). Validates batched matmul (attention core). code-first.

title: [perf][bench] grad benches measure tensor_randn+Session::new, not the backward (inflated hotspots)
description: ops_bench.rs grad_* benches (batch_norm grad_1d_8192x1024, layer_norm/rms_norm grad_2048x1024, group_norm grad_32x256x28x28) put FrankenTorchSession::new() + tensor_randn(LARGE) INSIDE b.iter(), unlike the nograd_* benches which hoist them. So the 'grad' medians in artifacts/perf/frankentorch-next-reprofile-20260617 (batch_norm 693ms, group_norm 494ms, batch_norm2d 513ms, layer_norm 165ms, rms_norm 156ms) are dominated by per-iter Gaussian RNG over 2-8.4M elements, NOT the backward kernel (batch_norm_backward_f64/f32 are ALREADY fully rayon-parallel). FIX: build the random inputs ONCE outside b.iter() as Vec<f64>, then per-iter rebuild requires_grad leaves via tensor_variable(data.clone(),..) (cheap memcpy, keeps a fresh tape) + forward + backward. Re-profile after to expose the REAL backward levers. Found by cc while scoping kgs4 batch_norm-grad: the kernel is already parallel so there was no kernel lever to chase.
close_reason: Fixed: grad benches now generate RNG once outside b.iter() + rebuild leaves via tensor_variable. batch_norm/grad_1d 693->262ms (2.65x) confirming the old number was ~62% per-iter randn, not the backward. Bench-only, no library change.

title: [parity] repeat_interleave only takes usize; torch supports a per-element repeats tensor
description: tensor_repeat_interleave(input, repeats: usize) only does the uniform-repeat form. torch.repeat_interleave(x, torch.tensor([1,2,3])) supports a per-element repeats tensor (and a dim arg). Add a variant tensor_repeat_interleave_tensor(input, repeats_tensor, dim) that expands each element by its repeat count. Found alongside masked_select broadcast (ucxgj). Lower priority.
close_reason: NON-BUG: tensor_repeat_interleave_tensor(input, repeats: &[usize], dim: Option<usize>) ALREADY supports per-element/per-slice repeats (verified matches torch: 1d [1,2,3]->[1,2,2,3,3,3]; dim0 [1,2]->[[1,2],[3,4],[3,4]]). The only difference vs torch is API form (Rust &[usize] slice vs a repeats TensorNodeId) — a capability is NOT missing. Filed too eagerly last session without checking the _tensor variant; closing.

comments.author: ubuntu
comments.text: Resolved by tensor_repeat_interleave_repeats (e2fc8ffa): the torch-faithful repeats-Tensor API form is now implemented (delegates to the existing &[usize] core which already had the capability). Verified vs torch 2.12 (1d/scalar-broadcast/dim/negative-error). 7 tests pass. (Bead was earlier closed as non-bug re: capability; this adds the missing API form.)

title: functional_normalize severs autograd; composable through abs/pow/sum_dim/div
description:
functional_normalize at lib.rs:5151 computes y = x / max(||x||_p along dim, eps) but the body extracts values, divides in plain f64, and rebuilds a requires_grad=false leaf — silently severing autograd through every L2-normalization layer. PyTorch's torch.nn.functional.normalize IS differentiable.

Compose through autograd primitives:
   abs_x   = tensor_abs(input)
   pow_x   = tensor_pow(abs_x, p)
   sum_x   = tensor_sum_dim(pow_x, dim)
   norm    = tensor_pow(sum_x, 1.0 / p)
   norm_c  = tensor_clamp_min(norm, eps)
   norm_kd = tensor_unsqueeze(norm_c, dim)
   norm_b  = tensor_expand(norm_kd, original_shape)
   y       = tensor_div(input, norm_b)

All ops are autograd-aware so gradients flow back to input. Sister of frankentorch-7yvv (dropout severance) — both are critical for training paths.
notes:
Fixed in this commit. Composed functional_normalize through abs/pow/sum_dim/clamp_min/reshape/expand/div primitives — same observable y = x / max(||x||_p along dim, eps) but with the autograd tape preserved.

The norm reduction is reshaped to a keepdim=true-style broadcast shape (input's shape with dim set to 1) so the subsequent expand + div work uniformly across 1-D and higher-rank inputs.

Added functional_normalize_propagates_gradient_through_input regression test for input=[3,4] L2-normalize on dim=0:
   y = [0.6, 0.8] under sum loss → grad = [(1 - y_i * sum_y) / ||x||_2] = [0.032, -0.024]
asserts to 1e-12.

title: Add end-to-end training example and integration test
description:
FrankenTorch has e2e training loop tests buried in ft-optim's test suite, but there is no standalone, documented example showing users how to train a model. This is critical for proving the system works end-to-end and for user onboarding.

Create a comprehensive integration test (in ft-conformance or a new examples/ directory) that:

1. Defines a 2-layer MLP: Linear(784, 128) -> ReLU -> Linear(128, 10)
2. Generates synthetic MNIST-like data using the existing TensorDataset/DataLoader
3. Trains for 5 epochs using Adam optimizer with learning rate scheduling (StepLR or CosineAnnealing)
4. Tracks loss per epoch and asserts it decreases monotonically (with tolerance for noise)
5. Uses no_grad context for evaluation pass
6. Saves model state_dict to file, loads it back, verifies predictions match
7. Uses BatchNorm or LayerNorm in the architecture to exercise normalization paths
8. Logs: epoch number, batch count, loss per epoch, final accuracy estimate, elapsed time

This test exercises the full stack: ft-data (DataLoader) -> ft-nn (modules) -> ft-api (forward/backward) -> ft-autograd (gradient computation) -> ft-optim (parameter update) -> ft-serialize (save/load).

If any component fails to compose correctly, this test catches it.

Tests:
- The example IS the test: it should be a #[test] fn in ft-conformance or similar
- Assert loss decreases over 5 epochs
- Assert save/load round-trip produces identical predictions
- Assert no_grad eval context doesn't corrupt gradient state
- Log detailed per-epoch metrics for debugging
close_reason: Added 5 E2E integration tests exercising the full stack: MLP regression with DataLoader/Adam/StepLR, binary classification with cross-entropy, save/load round-trip verification, no_grad eval safety, dataloader epoch shuffle. All pass.

title: conv2d groups= (functional + nn Conv2d) — grouped/depthwise convolution parity
description: functional_conv2d required weight_in_channels==in_channels (groups=1 only); nn Conv2d had no groups field. torch F.conv2d/nn.Conv2d support groups (depthwise=in_channels, grouped=ResNeXt/ShuffleNet). Implemented by composition (ft-api only, NO conv-kernel change): grouped conv = groups independent convs over disjoint channel slices, concatenated -> narrow(input ch)/narrow(weight out-ch)/narrow(bias) per group, functional_conv2d each, cat dim1. Bit-exact, autograd-aware. Added functional_conv2d_grouped + tensor alias; nn Conv2d::new_grouped (weight [out, in/groups, kh, kw]) + forward delegation for groups>1. Verified vs torch 2.12 (groups=2 + depthwise goldens, groups=1 identical, grad to all channels, divisibility rejection). Follow-up: conv2d dilation (zero-stuff weight), conv1d/3d groups.
close_reason: done

title: MaxUnpool1d/2d/3d sever autograd; scatter-then-gather backward needed
description:
MaxUnpool1d (line 6149), MaxUnpool2d (line 6237), and MaxUnpool3d (line 6337) all extract input values, scatter them via indices in plain f64, and rebuild requires_grad=false leaves. PyTorch's torch.nn.MaxUnpool* IS differentiable.

Backward: each output position came from a single input position (via the indices) — gather grad_output from those positions back to grad_input. Sister of repeat_interleave / diag_embed / unfold (gather-scatter pattern, except here forward is scatter and backward is gather, so the mapping inverts).

Each variant has the same forward/backward structure with different spatial indexing.
notes: Fixed in this commit. All three MaxUnpool variants (1d/2d/3d) now wrap their forward in tensor_apply_function with the analytical scatter-then-gather backward. The (input_pos → output_pos) indices mapping is captured by both forward and backward closures; backward gathers grad_output from the destination positions back to grad_input. ft-nn lib tests: 517.

title: ft-api: householder_product must reject m<n (torch parity bug) + verify ormqr/orgqr edge cases
description: torch.linalg.householder_product errors when shape[-2]<shape[-1] ('input.shape[-2] must be >= input.shape[-1]') but ft-api silently produced a wrong (m,n) result for wide reflector matrices. Added the m>=n guard. Also verified vs torch 2.x that the ACCEPTED edge cases are correct: orgqr with fewer reflectors than min(m,n) (tau shorter), and ormqr applied to a wide (m<n) reflector matrix (ormqr does accept m<n, unlike orgqr).
close_reason: Shipped 85381ec7. householder_product now rejects m<n (torch parity: 'input.shape[-2] must be >= input.shape[-1]'); was silently producing malformed output. Differential sweep confirmed accepted edge cases already correct (fewer-tau orgqr, m<n ormqr) vs torch goldens. ft-api suite 2044 pass.

title: MHA need_weights/average_attn_weights — return the attention matrix (torch parity)
description: nn.MultiheadAttention.forward returns (output, attn_weights) when need_weights=True (the default); FT's MHA returned only the output. Added forward_qkv_with_weights(q,k,v,attn_mask,is_causal,average_attn_weights) -> (output, weights). Output via the fused SDPA (== forward_qkv_masked); weights = softmax(scale*Q_h@K_h^T + mask) over the same projected heads, reshaped [N,heads,S_q,S_k], averaged over heads to [N,S_q,S_k] when average_attn_weights. attn_mask/is_causal honored in both. ft-nn only. Verified: output bit-matches forward_qkv_masked; weights rows sum to 1; causal weights lower-triangular; per-head shape when average=false. Follow-up: unify the 4 SDPA entry-point sigs; wire enable_gqa into MHA.
close_reason: done

title: [parity] ft-api: implement tensor_cfloat/cdouble (real<->complex casts)
description: GAP: tensor_cfloat()/tensor_cdouble() (.cfloat()/.cdouble()) always errored UnsupportedDType — a torch-parity gap. IMPL: compose through existing ops via a cast_real_to_complex helper — real input -> complex(input_cast, zeros) at target precision; complex input -> split (real/imag) + recast + recombine; already-target passthrough. Non-differentiable (inherits tensor_complex's requires_grad guard, consistent with fft/polar complex-autograd policy). Test cfloat_cdouble_cast_real_and_complex (real f64->C128, f32->C64, passthrough, C128->C64 downcast). No cross-crate changes.
close_reason: Implemented tensor_cfloat/cdouble via cast_real_to_complex composition (real->complex(cast,zeros); complex->split+recast+recombine; passthrough). ft-api only, no autograd/ft-core changes. Test cfloat_cdouble_cast_real_and_complex passes (f64->C128, f32->C64, passthrough, C128->C64 downcast). Closes a torch-parity gap (these always errored UnsupportedDType before). 1761 ft-api lib tests, clippy clean.

title: ft-api: cummax/cummin keep FIRST tied index; torch keeps LATEST (indices + gradient diverge)
description: tensor_cummax used strict > (and cummin strict <), so on ties it kept the FIRST occurrence of the running extremum. torch updates the index on EQUALITY (cummax >=, cummin <=), keeping the LATEST tied index: cummax([5,5,5]).indices = [0,1,2] (FT gave [0,0,0]); cummax([1,3,3,2,5]).indices = [0,1,2,2,4] (FT gave [0,1,1,1,4]). Verified torch 2.12. This diverged BOTH the indices output AND the values-branch GRADIENT (which routes through cum_idx): cummax([5,5,5]).values.sum().backward() = [1,1,1] in torch but FT gave [3,0,0]. FIXED: > -> >= and < -> <=. Existing tie-free tests unaffected. Golden cummax_cummin_tie_indices_match_torch. NOTE separate pre-existing NaN-propagation gap (FT cummax does not propagate NaN) NOT addressed here.
close_reason: SHIPPED 1eed6f6f. cummax > -> >= and cummin < -> <= so tied indices keep the LATEST occurrence (torch parity). Fixes both the indices output AND the values-branch gradient routing (cummax([5,5,5]) grad now [1,1,1] not [3,0,0]). Verified vs torch 2.12 (cummax_cummin_tie_indices_match_torch). Tie-free tests unaffected. ft-api 1931 pass. Pre-existing NaN-propagation gap noted separately.

title: [parity] ft-api: implement linalg_cholesky NxN autograd backward (lower factor)
description: GAP: torch.linalg.cholesky is differentiable but our NxN path failed loud (only 1x1 had a backward; frankentorch-rl4g). IMPL: standard differentiable-Cholesky (Murray 2016 / PyTorch cholesky_backward) in f64 via tensor_apply_function: phi=tril_half_diag(L^T@grad_L); A_bar=L^-T@phi@L^-1 (L^-1 via forward substitution); grad_A=(A_bar+A_bar^T)/2. Lower factor, square F64 (torch default); upper/non-F64 still fail loud. Verified by finite-difference gradient check (cholesky_nxn_backward_matches_finite_difference, directional derivative with symmetric perturbation). 16 cholesky tests pass.
close_reason: Implemented linalg_cholesky NxN autograd backward (lower factor, square F64) via the PyTorch cholesky_backward formula in f64: phi=tril_half_diag(L^T grad_L); A_bar=L^-T phi L^-1 (forward-substitution L^-1); grad_A=(A_bar+A_bar^T)/2. Helper cholesky_backward_lower. FD-verified (cholesky_nxn_backward_matches_finite_difference, directional derivative). upper/non-F64 still fail loud (bounded scope). 16 cholesky tests pass, clippy clean (no new warnings). Closes a real torch-parity autograd gap.

title: [parity] ft-api: linalg_cholesky upper-factor autograd backward
description: Extends the lower cholesky backward (frankentorch-cholesky-backward-wmef) to the UPPER factor. A=U^T U with U=L^T, so feed L=U^T and grad_L=grad_U^T to cholesky_backward_lower; grad_A is symmetric so no transpose-back. Unified the requires_grad branch to handle both upper/lower (square F64). FD-verified (cholesky_upper_backward_matches_finite_difference). 17 cholesky tests pass.
close_reason: Extended linalg_cholesky autograd backward to the upper factor: A=U^T U, U=L^T -> feed L=U^T, grad_L=grad_U^T to cholesky_backward_lower (grad_A symmetric, no transpose back). Unified requires_grad branch (upper+lower, square F64). FD-verified (cholesky_upper_backward_matches_finite_difference). 17 cholesky tests pass, clippy clean.

comments.author: RusticKite
comments.text: ROOT CAUSE: Session::{add,sub,mul,div}_scalar (ft-api/src/lib.rs ~55147) build the scalar via full(vec![1], scalar, false) which makes an F64 leaf, then call the binary op. tensor_add/sub/mul/div promote f32+f64 -> f64 (test f32_mixed_promotes_to_f64 @74748), so f32_tensor.add_scalar(2.0) silently returns F64. TORCH PARITY: python scalars are WEAK (torch.float32 tensor + 2.0 -> float32). Same bug class as tensor_mul_scalar (FIXED 908ef05f). Affects F32/F16/BF16 inputs (~2x memory + wrong dtype in f32 inference). FIX: cast the scalar leaf to the input's dtype before the binary op (F64 + non-float unchanged). No existing test pins the upcast.

title: ft-api: linalg_cond(p=±2) fails loud on requires_grad despite svdvals now being autograd-aware (torch parity)
description:
tensor_linalg_cond(p=2/-2) had a fail-loud requires_grad guard with a STALE rationale: it claimed tensor_linalg_svdvals severs the tape. That was true when the guard was written, but svdvals became autograd-aware (frankentorch-rl4g: grad_A = U diag(grad_σ) Vh). The composition svdvals -> narrow(sigma_max/min) -> div is fully autograd-aware, so the guard now needlessly rejects gradient-aware condition-number computations that torch.linalg.cond supports.

Fix: remove the guard + stale comment. Verified A=[[1,2],[3,4]] vs torch 2.12: cond2=14.9330343736593 grad=[31.1396,-20.5923,-12.0541,11.5518]; cond-2=0.0669656263407472 grad=[-0.139642,0.092344,0.054055,-0.051803]. title: ft-api: lp_pool 1d/2d/3d autograd (Lp-norm window backward)
description: functional_lp_pool1d/2d/3d silently severed autograd. Lp-pool out=(sum|x|^p)^(1/p) over each window is smooth; d out/dx = s^(1/p-1)|x|^(p-1)sign(x). Added shared lp_pool_grad_output(input, windows, p, out_shape): forward sums in caller order (bit-identical), backward scatter-adds the per-window derivative (overlapping windows accumulate). Each fn builds per-output window index lists in the requires_grad branch. FD-verified with overlapping windows. frankentorch-avfl-lppool.

title: ft-api: zeta autograd w.r.t. q (missed silent-sever, same class as igamma)
description: tensor_zeta (Hurwitz zeta, special.zeta) silently severed autograd. torch differentiates it w.r.t. q (the 2nd arg, parameter): dzeta(s,q)/dq = -s*zeta(s+1,q); s (1st arg, exponent) is non-differentiable (matches torch derivatives.yaml). 2-input apply_function returning [None, Some(grad_q)]. Bit-identical forward, FD-verified + closed-form -2*zeta(3,2). frankentorch-2vxa.
close_reason: Shipped commit 93d528bc: zeta autograd w.r.t. q (dzeta/dq=-s*zeta(s+1,q), s non-diff per torch). 2-input apply_function [None, Some(grad_q)]. Bit-exact forward + FD + closed-form verified. Pushed main+master.

title: [perf] cumprod forward: parallelize lanes (bit-exact) — resolves 66pe cumsum/cumprod puzzle
description: Parallelized cumprod_tensor_contiguous_f64 + _f32 forward with the SAME par_chunks_mut(lane) pattern already shipped+accepted for cumsum (independent outer lanes, per-lane multiplicative order unchanged => bit-for-bit identical; cumprod kernel tests pass). Resolves the open puzzle from 66pe/h5gj: an ANCHORED single-process A/B (cumprod_anchored_ab.rs) ran cumsum (accepted parallel win) as anchor + cumprod as question, BOTH serial+parallel, ONE process ONE worker. Healthy-worker runs: cumsum anchor 2.16-2.19x, cumprod 1.96-2.90x (cumprod tracks the anchor within ~0.9x). One contended run showed BOTH regress (cumsum 0.85x, cumprod 0.76x) — the anchor flags it as a bad-worker window, which is exactly the un-anchored confound that produced the original 66pe '1.83x regression' rejection. Gated numel>=PARALLEL_THRESHOLD && outer_size>=2.
close_reason: SHIPPED. cumprod fwd parallel (f64+f32), bit-exact (mirrors accepted cumsum), anchored A/B proves prior 66pe regression was worker-variance not a real regression.

title: ft-nn loss modules sever autograd: HingeEmbeddingLoss, FocalLoss, MultiMarginLoss, others
description:
Multiple loss modules in ft-nn extract values, compute the per-element loss in plain f64, and rebuild a non-grad leaf via tensor_variable(_, _, false). The subsequent reduction (mean/sum/none) operates on this severed leaf so gradients never reach the input.

Affected (line numbers from current main):
  - HingeEmbeddingLoss::forward_hinge (lib.rs:11003)
  - FocalLoss / its forward (lib.rs:~11800, near 11810)
  - MultiMarginLoss (lib.rs:~11827)
  - Other reduction-style loss modules in the 11800-12100 range

PyTorch's torch.nn.HingeEmbeddingLoss / FocalLoss / MultiMarginLoss are all differentiable. Each loss has a closed-form composition through autograd-aware primitives.

Children: each loss gets its own fix bead. Sister of frankentorch-2med (the session-method loss umbrella that closed earlier).
notes: Children landed: HingeEmbeddingLoss (8530), MultiMarginLoss (1s4q), SoftMarginLoss (fjao), FocalLoss multi-class softmax variant (kxcz), MultiLabelMarginLoss (r38w). Each loss module now composes through autograd-aware primitives instead of severing via tensor_values + tensor_variable(false).

title: ft-autograd: cholesky_solve grad wrt the factor L is masked to the triangle; torch returns a DENSE factor gradient
description: torch.cholesky_solve(b,L).sum().backward() gives a dense grad_L (e.g. [[-0.385695,-0.658984],[-1.285649,-1.628078]] for L=[[1.41421,0],[0.35355,1.17260]]) — the off-triangle entry L[0,1] gets -0.658984 even though the FORWARD ignores it (verified: perturbing L[0,1] does not change the output). ft composes cholesky_solve via two masked triangular_solves, so ft's grad_L is masked (0 at L[0,1]). ft's value is arguably 'more correct' (true grad wrt an ignored input is 0) but DIVERGES from torch's convention. PRE-EXISTING (1st-order), surfaced while adding triangular_solve 2nd-order (nj7x8). Decide: match torch's dense convention (un-mask the cholesky_solve factor gradient) or keep the mathematically-exact masked grad. Probe: /tmp/chol_solve_check.py pattern. NOTE torch.linalg.solve_triangular DOES mask (ft matches it there) — the dense convention is specific to cholesky_solve.
close_reason: Already implemented (ed1b988d): cholesky_solve is a primitive autograd op carrying torch's dense cholesky_solve_backward (grad_L = -(common·L), common = sym(grad_B·X^T)). Verified ft grad_L matches torch bit-for-bit across lower 2x2 / upper 2x2 / lower 3x3 incl off-triangle entries (examples/crvxw_check.rs ALL_MATCH=true). Suite 2112 pass.

title: [perf][no-gaps] ft-kernel-cpu: qr_contiguous_f64 is BLAS-2 (~60x off LAPACK, 176ms@512) — block it with compact-WY GEMM trailing update (dgeqrf/dlarfb)
notes: NB tuning NEGATIVE RESULT (BoldFalcon): NB=32->64 REGRESSED the blocked QR (qr_512 53->84ms, tall_2048x128 27->52ms). So NB=32 is near-optimal and the QR panel FACTORIZATION (BLAS-2 scalar Householder within the panel) is the remaining bottleneck, not GEMM skinniness. Square QR at ~4.5 GFLOP/s vs matrixmultiply 85 -> the panel factor + small per-panel GEMMs cap it. Further QR speedup needs RECURSIVE panel factorization (block the panel factor itself) — complex; deprioritized vs the Hessenberg (9y5bi). QR considered DONE for now (3.5x square + 4-5.5x tall).
close_reason: Completed recursive QR panel factorization: vmi1227854 qr_f64_512x512 67.182->45.689 ms (1.47x), qr_f64_tall_2048x128 46.387->24.812 ms (1.87x); focused QR tests and proof bundle passed.

title: ft-api: Chebyshev u/v/w + shifted autograd (completes ortho-poly family)
description: The 7 remaining Chebyshev special fns (chebyshev u/v/w, shifted t/u/v/w) silently severed autograd. Added free chebyshev_u_deriv helper (U_n'=((n+1)T_{n+1}-x U_n)/(x^2-1)); V_n'=U_n'-U_{n-1}', W_n'=U_n'+U_{n-1}' (via V=U_n-U_{n-1}, W=U_n+U_{n-1}); shifted = chain rule x2 on 2x-1. All via special_unary_n_with_deriv. Bit-identical forward (par_map_f64 preserved), FD-verified all 7 (confirms scalar conventions). Completes the entire orthogonal-polynomial autograd family. frankentorch-avfl-cheby.

Added tensor_tensordot_dims(a,b,a_dims,b_dims) built from an einsum equation (so grads flow) — output = a's free axes then b's free axes, matching torch; negatives allowed. Verified vs torch fwd (incl reordered) + bwd.

Output = a free axes then b free axes (torch order); negatives allowed; mismatched lengths error. title: ft-autograd: norm/dist p=1 backward uses f64::signum (±1 at 0) not torch sign (0 at 0)
description: The Norm/NormDim backward (tensor_norm, tensor_dist, tensor_linalg_norm) computed d|x|/dx via Rust f64::signum, which returns +1 at +0.0 and -1 at -0.0. torch's subgradient is sign(0)=0, so the p=1 (and general-p / inf) norm gradient diverged at any element exactly equal across the diff. Found via differential gradient test vs torch 2.12: dist([1,2,3],[4,6,3],p=1) grad expected [-1,-1,0] but FrankenTorch gave [-1,-1,1]. FIXED: added Self::torch_sign (0 at +/-0, NaN propagates, same convention as the Abs backward) and replaced all 8 .signum() sites in Norm/NormDim backward (regular + create_graph). Golden test session_backward_grads_match_torch covers det/logdet/trace/cross/dist(p1,p3)/vector_norm(p1,p3)/cummax/lerp/addcmul/diff.
close_reason: SHIPPED 826cde6f. Added Self::torch_sign (0 at +/-0, NaN propagates) and replaced all 8 f64::signum sites in Norm/NormDim backward (regular + create_graph). dist/norm p=1 gradient now matches torch at exactly-equal elements. Found + verified via session_backward_grads_match_torch (13 ops). ft-autograd 436 + ft-api 1921 pass.

title: tensor_diag_embed is severed-autograd: linear backward, easy fix
description:
tensor_diag_embed at lib.rs:3444 places a 1-D input vector onto the diagonal (or sub/super-diagonal) of a zero-initialized 2-D matrix. PyTorch's torch.diag_embed is differentiable. The forward is purely linear in the input:
   output[row, col] = input[i] if (row, col) is on the requested diagonal else 0
so the backward is just the diagonal-extraction:
   grad_input[i] = grad_output[row, col]
where (row, col) is the same position the forward placed input[i] in.

Wrap in tensor_apply_function with this analytical backward. Save offset and k for backward; or capture via closure. Surfaced during /reality-check pass scanning for the values-then-non-grad-leaf pattern.
notes: Fixed in this commit. Wrapped tensor_diag_embed in tensor_apply_function with diagonal-extraction backward: grad_input[i] = grad_output[row, col] for the same (row, col) the forward placed input[i]. Captured offset (positive vs negative) and abs_offset via closure (Copy-by-move). Added diag_embed_propagates_gradient_via_diagonal_extraction regression test using a weighted sum over a 3x3 main-diagonal embedding (input [1,2,3] gets gradient [1,5,9] = weights[i,i]). ft-api lib tests: 1145.

title: tensor_combinations severs autograd; gather-then-scatter backward
description:
tensor_combinations at lib.rs:4169 enumerates r-element combinations from a 1-D input (with or without replacement). Each output cell is a copy of one input element via a combinatorial mapping. PyTorch's torch.combinations IS differentiable.

Same pattern as cartesian_prod / unfold: pre-compute the (output_pos → input_pos) mapping during the recursive combo enumeration, then wrap in tensor_apply_function with gather-scatter backward.
notes: Fixed in this commit. Wrapped tensor_combinations in tensor_apply_function with gather-then-scatter backward. Pre-computed (output_pos → input_pos) mapping during the recursive combo enumeration (using indices instead of values), captured in both forward and backward closures. Added combinations_propagates_gradient_via_scatter regression test (input=[10,20,30], r=2 no-replacement → 3 combos, each input appears in 2 rows → grad=[2,2,2]). ft-api lib tests: 1168.

title: tensor_linalg_pinv: autograd via normal-equation formulation for full-rank A
description:
tensor_linalg_pinv in ft-api (line 12640) calls tensor_linalg_svd internally which now fails loud on requires_grad inputs (frankentorch-rl4g). This breaks pinv in autograd flows entirely.

Implement an autograd-aware pinv path for full-rank A:
- m >= n (full column rank): A^+ = (A^T A)^{-1} A^T
- m <= n (full row rank): A^+ = A^T (A A^T)^{-1}

Both compose through tensor_transpose + tensor_matmul + tensor_linalg_inv (all autograd-aware after recent fixes). The SVD-based path stays for non-grad inputs (handles rank-deficient cases properly).

Affects: crates/ft-api/src/lib.rs tensor_linalg_pinv (line 12640).

title: [bug][parity] ft-api bincount errors on f32: feeds f32 input to tensor_scatter_add as index tensor

Test bincount_supports_f32_input passes; all 11 bincount + ft-api 1886 green. Pushed main+master.

comments.author: RusticKite
comments.text: bincount(f32) errors UnsupportedDType(F32): its value read (now routable to tensor_values_lossy_f64) is only half — the unweighted path calls tensor_scatter_add(zeros, 0, input, ones) with the f32 INPUT as the index tensor, and scatter_add requires f64 indices. title: tensor_scatter_reduce severs autograd via tensor_values + non-grad rebuild
description:
tensor_scatter_reduce in ft-api (line 16249) extracts both input and src tensor_values, runs the reduction in plain f64, and rebuilds via tensor_variable(_, _, false). Silently severs autograd. PyTorch supports gradients for reduce='sum'/'mean'/'max'/'min'.

Proper fix: compose the 'sum' and 'mean' modes via tensor_scatter_add (autograd-aware) — sum with the source, then divide by (1 + count) for mean. 'max'/'min' modes need argmax-routing via apply_function.

Interim fix: add a fail-loud guard on requires_grad input/src so the silent severance becomes loud.

Affects: crates/ft-api/src/lib.rs tensor_scatter_reduce (line 16249).
